perturbation.original_text: Subject to section 5.4, within 10 days of a written request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall, unless otherwise precluded by any legal obligation: (a) return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all of the Disclosing Party’s original tangible Confidential Information, together with all ta ngible copies and reproductions; (b) subject to subsection 5.3(c) , make its best efforts to delete all electronic copies of the Confidential Information received from the Disclosing Party; (c) safeguard any electronic Confidential Information that cannot be destroyed due to the Receiving Party’s archiving practices or policies with the same degree of care as it would its own Confidential Information; and
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to section 5.4, within 1 day of a written request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall, unless otherwise precluded by any legal obligation: (a) return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all of the Disclosing Party’s original tangible Confidential Information, together with all ta ngible copies and reproductions; (b) subject to subsection 5.3(c) , make its best efforts to delete all electronic copies of the Confidential Information received from the Disclosing Party; (c) safeguard any electronic Confidential Information that cannot be destroyed due to the Receiving Party’s archiving practices or policies with the same degree of care as it would its own Confidential Information; and
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return/destruction deadline to 1 day may be unreasonably short under certain legal standards and thus creates a legal inconsistency.
perturbation.law_citation: Limitations Act, RSA 2000, c L-12, s 3(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: Alberta's Limitations Act generally provides a limitation period of two years for commencing a claim. While this section doesn't directly deal with the limitation period, the reasonableness of a one-day period could be challenged in court. A very short period, like one day, may be deemed unreasonable depending on the circumstances and could be argued as conflicting with the spirit of allowing a fair opportunity to address legal matters.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that changing the return/destruction deadline to 1 day may be unreasonably short under certain legal standards and thus creates a legal inconsistency. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction', which focuses on time-sensitive obligations that do not align with legal requirements. The law explanation further supports this by mentioning that a very short period, like one day, may be deemed unreasonable depending on the circumstances and could be argued as conflicting with the spirit of allowing a fair opportunity to address legal matters. Snippet 1's gibberish is unhelpful, but the combination of the perturbation explanation, law explanation and the definition of the perturbation type justifies the existence of a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Should either Party change its address while the Agreement is in effect, it is the obligation of that Party to provide notice to the other Party, in writing, of its new address and the date the new address is effective prior to changing its address.
perturbation.changed_text: Should either Party change its address while the Agreement is in effect, it is the obligation of that Party to provide notice to the other Party, in writing, of its new address and the date the new address is effective 30 days after changing its address.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification of address change for 30 days after the change is ineffective creates a legal risk, especially in cases where legal notices or important communications need to be delivered promptly. The receiving party is at a disadvantage for a month in this scenario.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding reasonable notice
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Although there isn't a precise statute defining the timeframe for providing a change of address, delaying notification by 30 days may violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in contract law. The duty implies that parties must not act in a way that deprives the other party of the benefits of the agreement. Delaying important information such as a change of address can harm the other party's ability to enforce their rights or receive vital communications.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a delay in notifying the other party about a change of address, which could be interpreted as a violation of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The original text requires notification 'prior to changing' the address, while the modified text delays it '30 days after changing'. The Law Explanation states that delaying important information such as a change of address can harm the other party's ability to enforce their rights or receive vital communications. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'faithful performance of duties,' suggesting that delaying important information could be seen as a breach of this duty. This aligns with the definition of inconsistencies as violations of time-sensitive obligations, because delaying the notification for 30 days after the change is a violation of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted under the laws of the Province of Alberta and the Parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Province of Alberta for all disputes arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted under the laws of the Republic of China and the Parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Republic of China for all disputes arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and jurisdiction to the Republic of China (Taiwan) may create enforceability issues and contradict expectations if the parties are operating primarily within Alberta, Canada. It could also affect the interpretation of the agreement based on potentially differing legal standards.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Conflict of Laws principles related to jurisdiction and choice of law
perturbation.law_citation: Hague Choice of Court Agreements Convention
perturbation.law_explanation: While Alberta's laws permit choice of law provisions, this is subject to conflict of laws principles and potential public policy exceptions. If the agreement has no reasonable connection to China, a court may refuse to enforce the choice of law or jurisdiction provision, especially if it prejudices one of the parties. Alberta is not a contracting state to the Hague Choice of Court Agreements Convention.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Principle V.1.4 - Principle of simultaneous performance; right to withhold performance | Trans-Lex.org This page uses so called "cookies" to improve its service (i.e. "tracking"). Learn more and opt out of tracking I agree Principles 1672 Bibliography 1035 Materials 86 Archive 66 History & Modern Evolution of Transnational Commercial Law Prof. Dr. Klaus Peter Berger, LL.M. transnational law transnational law (lex mercatoria or international business law) and "Principle of simultaneous performance; right to withhold performance" 2016-03-01 13:58:16 https://www.trans-lex.org/img/logo_ball.png Document-Id: 941000, Please cite as: "https://www.trans-lex.org/941000" Principle No. V.1.4 - Principle of simultaneous performance; right to withhold performance (a) The parties to a bilateral contract are required to render their respective performances simultaneously, unless they have agreed on or circumstances indicate a different order of performance. (b) If performance of an obligation by one party is dependent upon performance of another obligation arising out of the same contract by the other party, each party may withhold performance until tender of performance by the other party. Where one party has performed in part, the other party cannot refuse to perform if to do so would be contrary to good faith, having regard in particular to the relatively slight or trivial nature of the default (" exceptio non adimpleti contractus ", " inadimplenti non est adimplendum "). Commentary References Contract Clauses Commentary 1 A scenario in which the parties are not under a duty to perform simultaneously as provided for in Subsection (a) would be a contract which provides for delivery and deferred payment or delivery on credit or, vice-versa , for payment in advance of delivery. 2 The question as to whether the performance of an obligation is dependent upon performance of another obligation by the other party as provided for in Subsection (b) is a matter of contract interpretation. This always applies to those core obligations of a contract which constitute a direct exchange of promises and which are characterized in some jurisdictions as "synallagmatic" or "reciprocal" (" do ut des "), i.e. the duty of the seller to deliver the goods sold and the duty of the buyer to pay the sales price. There may be contracts in which two obligations are so closely connected by the will of the parties as expressed in the contract that they may be qualified as "dependent" even though they would not be characterized as "synallagmatic" or "reciprocal" in those jurisdictions. 3 Subsection (b) makes it clear that the exercise of the right to withhold performance is always subject to the Principle of good faith ( Trans-Lex Principle I.1.1 ). The principle of proportionality mentioned in Subsection (b) is derived from the Principle of good faith. It would be contrary to good faith (and amount to an abuse of right ) to rely on the non-performance of a relatively minor obligation by the other side in an attempt to avoid performance of an essential obligation of the party invoking the right to withhold performance, see Fouchard Gaillard Goldman On International Commercial Arbitration, 1999, No. 1486. 4 The relative triviality of non-performance by one party, however, is but one illustration of a more general restriction of the scope of subsection (b) by the requirement of good faith. The overriding Principle of good faith may restrict a party's right to invoke a right to withhold performance also in other scenarios outside the ambit of Subsection (b). Please cite as: "Commentary to Trans-Lex Principle , https://www.trans-lex.org/941000 " References Jump to: Please select a chapter! perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Alberta law and jurisdiction. The changed text replaces this with the law and jurisdiction of the Republic of China. The Perturbation Explanation highlights potential enforceability issues and differing legal standards. The Law Explanation states that a court may refuse to enforce the choice of law or jurisdiction provision if the agreement has no reasonable connection to China. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction,' where changing a time-sensitive obligation (jurisdiction and governing law) violates federal or state law by potentially making the contractual terms unenforceable, specifically if there is no connection to China. Snippet 2 is irrelevant.
